messages.content:
=== MOONSHINER TASK BOUNDARY ===

Platform team here. Every shared lab box has a slightly different, hand-rolled copy of our shell/editor config and it is misery to support. The plan: keep the blessed files in one directory checked out from git (the 'kit') and SYMLINK them into place, so updating the kit updates every box. I need the installer done properly — people have real, personalized files sitting where our links want to go, and we are not allowed to destroy them. One script: `dots.sh`, repo root, plain bash. It runs on the macOS minis too, so portable commands only — in particular there is no readlink -f there, and you don't need it: `[ -L ]` and plain `readlink` cover everything. Pin LC_ALL=C in the script; the manifest order below must be locale-proof.

Usage: `dots.sh install <srcdir> <targetdir>` and `dots.sh uninstall <srcdir> <targetdir>`.

The kit is every top-level regular file in <srcdir> — dotfiles very much included, subdirectories ignored. Both dirs must already exist. Process kit files in C byte order of their names. Links must be ABSOLUTE — callers invoke this from anywhere with relative paths, and a link that only resolves from one cwd is a broken link; resolve <srcdir> to an absolute path yourself (cd + pwd is fine) so that `readlink` on an installed link prints `<abs-srcdir>/<name>`.

install, per kit file <name>, target path <targetdir>/<name>:
- already a symlink to exactly our source -> line `ok <name>`, touch nothing
- a symlink pointing anywhere else -> replace it with ours, line `relink <name>` (links are never backed up)
- a regular file -> move it to <targetdir>/dotbackup/<name> (create dotbackup on demand), then link, line `backup+link <name>`
- nothing there -> link, line `link <name>`
After the per-file lines: `done: <n> changed, <m> ok`. Second install right after a first must be all `ok` with `done: 0 changed, 4 ok` (for a 4-file kit) and must NOT touch the backups again.

Safety preflight, before ANY filesystem change: if any target is a directory -> `dots.sh: target is a directory: <name>` to stderr, exit 73; if any target is a regular file whose backup slot <targetdir>/dotbackup/<name> is already occupied -> `dots.sh: backup already exists: <name>` to stderr, exit 73. Either way: stdout empty, nothing changed at all — a half-installed kit is worse than none.

uninstall, per kit file, C order: if the target is a symlink pointing to exactly our source, remove it, and if dotbackup/<name> exists move it back into place -> line `restore <name>`, otherwise -> line `unlink <name>`. Anything else (missing, a regular file, someone else's link) -> line `skip <name>`, hands off. Then `done: <n> changed, <m> skipped`. Remove dotbackup afterwards if it's empty; leave it if not. Files in the target dir that aren't in the kit are never touched by anything, ever.

Errors: unknown subcommand or wrong arg count -> `usage: dots.sh install|uninstall <srcdir> <targetdir>` on stderr, exit 64. Either dir missing -> `dots.sh: not a directory: <arg>`, exit 66. A kit with zero top-level regular files -> `dots.sh: nothing to manage in: <srcdir>`, exit 65. Success runs print nothing to stderr and exit 0.

`bash test_dots.sh` from the workspace root is the acceptance contract — it must pass unmodified, don't touch it.

Now write dots.sh:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# dots.sh -- install/uninstall a dotfiles kit as absolute symlinks.
#
#   dots.sh install   <srcdir> <targetdir>
#   dots.sh uninstall <srcdir> <targetdir>
#
# The kit is every top-level regular file in <srcdir> (dotfiles included,
# subdirectories ignored), processed in C byte order of their names.

LC_ALL=C
export LC_ALL
unset CDPATH

usage() {
  printf 'usage: dots.sh install|uninstall <srcdir> <targetdir>\n' >&2
  exit 64
}

# ---- argument validation ---------------------------------------------------

[ "$#" -eq 3 ] || usage
cmd=$1
srcdir=$2
targetdir=$3
case "$cmd" in
  install|uninstall) ;;
  *) usage ;;
esac

if [ ! -d "$srcdir" ]; then
  printf 'dots.sh: not a directory: %s\n' "$srcdir" >&2
  exit 66
fi
if [ ! -d "$targetdir" ]; then
  printf 'dots.sh: not a directory: %s\n' "$targetdir" >&2
  exit 66
fi

# Resolve the kit directory to an absolute path so installed links are
# absolute and resolve from any cwd.
abs_src=$(cd -- "$srcdir" && pwd) || exit 1

# ---- collect the kit: top-level regular files, C byte order -----------------

shopt -s nullglob dotglob
kit=()
for path in "$abs_src"/*; do
  [ -f "$path" ] && [ ! -L "$path" ] || continue
  kit+=("${path##*/}")
done

if [ "${#kit[@]}" -eq 0 ]; then
  printf 'dots.sh: nothing to manage in: %s\n' "$srcdir" >&2
  exit 65
fi

backupdir=$targetdir/dotbackup

case "$cmd" in
  install)
    # Safety preflight: refuse to change anything if any target is a
    # directory or any needed backup slot is already occupied.
    for name in "${kit[@]}"; do
      target=$targetdir/$name
      if [ -d "$target" ] && [ ! -L "$target" ]; then
        printf 'dots.sh: target is a directory: %s\n' "$name" >&2
        exit 73
      fi
    done
    for name in "${kit[@]}"; do
      target=$targetdir/$name
      if [ -f "$target" ] && [ ! -L "$target" ] && { [ -e "$backupdir/$name" ] || [ -L "$backupdir/$name" ]; }; then
        printf 'dots.sh: backup already exists: %s\n' "$name" >&2
        exit 73
      fi
    done

    changed=0
    ok=0
    for name in "${kit[@]}"; do
      src=$abs_src/$name
      target=$targetdir/$name
      if [ -L "$target" ]; then
        if [ "$(readlink "$target")" = "$src" ]; then
          printf 'ok %s\n' "$name"
          ok=$((ok + 1))
        else
          rm -- "$target"
          ln -s "$src" "$target"
          printf 'relink %s\n' "$name"
          changed=$((changed + 1))
        fi
      elif [ -e "$target" ]; then
        mkdir -p -- "$backupdir"
        mv -- "$target" "$backupdir/$name"
        ln -s "$src" "$target"
        printf 'backup+link %s\n' "$name"
        changed=$((changed + 1))
      else
        ln -s "$src" "$target"
        printf 'link %s\n' "$name"
        changed=$((changed + 1))
      fi
    done
    printf 'done: %d changed, %d ok\n' "$changed" "$ok"
```

**What was built** — `dots.sh` at the repo root, plain bash, `LC_ALL=C` pinned and exported at the top so kit enumeration via `shopt -s dotglob nullglob` + `"$abs_src"/*` globbing comes out in C byte order (dotfiles included, subdirs and symlinks excluded).

Key design points:
- **Absolute links without `readlink -f`**: srcdir is resolved with `abs_src=$(cd -- "$srcdir" && pwd)` in a subshell (main shell never changes cwd, so the relative targetdir stays valid), and every link is `ln -s "$abs_src/$name"`, so `readlink` prints the absolute kit path from any caller cwd. `CDPATH` is unset so the `cd` can't print noise.
- **Install** per file: exact-ours symlink → `ok`; any other symlink → `rm` + `ln -s` → `relink` (never backed up); regular file → `mkdir -p dotbackup` on demand, `mv`, link → `backup+link`; absent → `link`. Ends with `done: <n> changed, <m> ok`.
- **Atomic preflight**: two full passes over the kit before touching anything — first directory targets (`[ -d ] && ![ -L ]`, so a symlink *to* a dir is still treated as a relinkable symlink), then regular-file targets whose `dotbackup/<name>` slot is occupied. First conflict prints `dots.sh: target is a directory: <name>` / `dots.sh: backup already exists: <name>` to stderr and exits 73 with stdout empty and zero filesystem changes.
- **Uninstall**: only symlinks pointing exactly at our source are removed — backup restored (`restore`) or not (`unlink`); everything else (missing, real files, foreign links, bystanders) is `skip`. `rmdir dotbackup 2>/dev/null || :` afterwards removes it only if empty. Ends with `done: <n> changed, <m> skipped`.
- **Errors**: wrong arg count or unknown subcommand → usage on stderr, 64; missing srcdir/targetdir (checked in that order, reported as passed) → 66; empty kit (after both dir checks) → 65. Success writes nothing to stderr and exits 0.

Beyond the harness, I verified with a throwaway probe (since removed) that paths with spaces, kit subdirectories-with-files, and namespaced invocations all behave: links absolute, subdir contents ignored, uninstall leaves the home dir empty.

Storage ticket. The render-farm NAS keeps filling up and every time it does, three people ssh in and run three different du incantations with three different block sizes and we argue about whose numbers are right. I want this settled with one checked-in tool: `dureport.sh`, repo root, bash + awk, and it has to run on the two old macOS boxes in the machine room as well as the Linux fileservers — portable flags only, and pin LC_ALL=C inside the script so nobody's locale wobbles the output.

Important wrinkle: the script does NOT run du itself. The NAS cron captures `du -k` for each volume into a dump file nightly, and we analyze those dumps offline (often days later, often on a different machine). Your input is the dump. Format is exactly what `du -k` emits: one directory per line, `<kbytes><TAB><path>`, sizes are cumulative KiB, children listed before their parent, and the final line is the scan root itself. Paths never contain tabs (spaces are fair game — we have plenty). The dumps are machine-produced; the only corruption we ever see is truncation garbage, so: any line without a tab, with a non-numeric size field, or with an empty path is `dureport.sh: bad du record at line <n>` to stderr, exit 65. A zero-line dump is `dureport.sh: empty du dump`, exit 65.

Usage: `dureport.sh [--top N] [--depth D] <dufile|->` — `-` means read the dump from stdin. Flags and the filename may appear in any order. N and D must be positive decimal integers; defaults N=5, D=2. Anything wrong with the invocation (unknown flag, flag missing its value, bad N or D, zero, no dufile, two dufiles) prints `usage: dureport.sh [--top N] [--depth D] <dufile|->` to stderr and exits 64. A missing/unreadable dump file is `dureport.sh: cannot read: <arg>` to stderr, exit 66. Success: report on stdout, nothing on stderr, exit 0.

The report, byte-exact:

TOTAL<TAB><human>
DIRS (depth<=D)
<path><TAB><human>   ... every directory except the root itself whose depth relative to the root is <= D, sorted by path ascending in plain C byte order
TOP N
<path><TAB><human>   ... the N largest directories from the WHOLE dump at any depth (root excluded), largest first

TOTAL is the root line's size. Depth: a direct child of the root is depth 1, its child is depth 2, etc. TOP ordering is by exact KiB descending — never by the rounded display value — with ties broken by path ascending in C byte order, including when a tie group straddles the N cutoff. Fewer than N directories means fewer rows. Both section headers always print, even when a section has no rows (a one-line dump reports just TOTAL and the two bare headers).

Human sizes: do the math in awk — no bc, no shelling back out. Under 1024 KiB print integer KiB like `512K`. From 1024 KiB up to but not including 1048576 KiB print mebibytes with exactly one decimal, like `1.5M`. From 1048576 KiB print gibibytes with exactly one decimal, like `1.9G`. The decimal is rounded HALF UP at one decimal place: 1280 KiB is 1.25M exactly and must print `1.3M`, not `1.2M`. Yes, that means 1048575 KiB prints as `1024.0M` — that's the spec, the unit switches on the raw KiB threshold, not on the rounded value.

messages.content:
Everything checks out. The probe also confirms the script pins its own locale (ran under `fr_FR.UTF-8`, output still C-locale), paths with spaces sort in C byte order, and a `.` scan root is handled. No mawk/busybox on this box, but the awk program deliberately uses only POSIX features (hand-rolled merge sort instead of gawk's `asort`, no `gensub`), so it runs on the old BWK awk shipped with macOS.

## Summary

**Deliverable:** `dureport.sh` at the repo root (bash + awk, `LC_ALL=C` pinned via `export` inside the script). It never runs `du` — it analyzes the nightly `du -k` dump from a file or stdin (`-`).

**Design decisions that settle the "whose numbers are right" argument:**
- **One awk pass** validates and stores every record; the report is emitted only from `END`, so a corrupt dump can never leak partial output to stdout (validation failures exit 65 with an empty stdout, as the spec demands).
- **Half-up rounding in pure integer awk math** — `floor((10k + 512)/1024)` tenths for MiB, `floor((10k + 524288)/1048576)` for GiB — avoiding C `printf`'s round-half-to-even, which would print `1.2M` for 1280 KiB. Unit thresholds switch on raw KiB, so 1048575 KiB → `1024.0M` per spec.
- **Exact-KiB TOP ordering** with an explicit path-ascending tie-break comparator, so tie groups straddling the `--top N` cutoff resolve deterministically (the pool fixture: `mid`, `alpha`, `delta`).
- **Sorting is a merge sort written in awk** (O(n log n), no temp files, no reliance on GNU-vs-BSD `sort` flag quirks); string comparisons are forced (`path[a] ""`) so numeric-looking paths can't compare numerically, and with `LC_ALL=C` they're plain byte order.
- **Depth** is computed by stripping the root prefix and counting remaining components, which also handles roots like `.` and `/`.
- Exit codes: 64 usage / 65 bad-or-empty dump / 66 unreadable file, with the exact byte-for-byte messages from the ticket.

**Proof:** `bash test_dureport.sh` (unmodified) — `SUMMARY: all 64 checks passed`.
messages.reasoning_content:
No mawk/busybox available locally, but the script uses only POSIX awk features (functions, arrays, substr/index/gsub/sub, printf with redirection, exit codes, no gawk extensions like asort/gensub), so it will run on BWK awk on old macOS. Locale test: ran under fr_FR.UTF-8 externally and output is still correct C-locale output since the script pins LC_ALL=C itself.

Probe verified: a space-containing path sorts correctly ('./a' before './a b' in C byte order — space 0x20 < end-of-string... wait './a' is a prefix of './a b', shorter sorts first ✓), root '.' handling works, half-up 1536 KiB = 1.5M ✓.

Everything is done. Summarize.

=== MOONSHINER TASK BOUNDARY ===

The morning feed-round script is the last one in the barn repo still failing the new shellcheck CI step (style severity, zero findings), and the crew has real complaints to go with the red build: Willow's chart note says the bran mash is a 50\50 mix with hot water but the printed sheet says '5050', which confused the weekend volunteer; the sheet also splits the note onto the wrong side of the | separators some mornings; the 'short on:' summary comes out glued together with colons instead of spaces; and last week the sheet claimed we had plain salt in the bin room when all we actually stock is sea salt, so Pip got nothing. The acceptance gate is `bash test_feedmix.sh` — it runs the lint, then compares the prep sheet byte-for-byte against the sheets the barn manager signed off on. Please make it all pass without changing the chart format, the sheet layout, or the exit codes. test_feedmix.sh is read-only.

messages.reasoning_content:
Let me understand the task:

1. The test runs `shellcheck -S style feedmix.sh` — must be zero findings.
2. Then it compares output byte-for-byte against expected sheets.

Issues in `feedmix.sh`:

1. `warn() { printf '%s\n' $@ >&2 }` — unquoted `$@` (SC2048/SC2068). Should be `"$@"`. That fixes the usage message potentially being word-split. Actually with a single argument it's fine, but shellcheck will complain. Fix: `printf '%s\n' "$@" >&2`.

2. `while read name scoops feed sup note; do` — `read` without `-r` will mangle backslashes (SC2162). Willow's note `50\50 with hot water` — without `-r`, `read` eats the backslash, producing `5050 with hot water`. That's the "5050" complaint. Fix: `read -r`.

3. `IFS=:` set before the while loop persists for the rest of the script. That affects `"${missing[*]}"` — the `*` join uses the first char of IFS, so `short on:` line gets joined with `:` instead of space. That's the "glued together with colons" complaint. Fix: set IFS only for the read command: `while IFS=: read -r name scoops feed sup note; do`. That way IFS stays default for `"${missing[*]}"`.

4. `print_row ${row[@]}` — unquoted array expansion (SC2068? Actually SC2048 for `${array[@]}` unquoted). More importantly, word splitting on the note "50\50 with hot water" would split it into separate parts, so the note gets split onto the wrong side of the `|` separators: "note: 50\50 | with | hot | water". Fix: `print_row "${row[@]}"`.

5. `have_supplement()`: `if [[ " ${stocked[@]} " =~ " $1 " ]]` — this regex-quoted comparison... Let me think. In bash, `[[ string =~ regex ]]` where the regex is quoted makes it a literal string match. `" ${stocked[@]} "` with the array inside double quotes joins elements with the first char of IFS (space by default). So `" ${stocked[@]} "` = " sea salt flax ". And `" $1 "` = " sea salt ". The `=~` with a quoted right side does substring matching literally. So " sea salt " is a substring of " sea salt flax " → match. That seems to work for multi-word supplements...

Wait, but there's a bug: the complaint is "the sheet claimed we had plain salt in the bin room when all we actually stock is sea salt". Let's trace: stocked=("sea salt" "flax"). `" ${stocked[@]} "` = " sea salt flax ". For sup="salt", `" $1 "` = " salt ". Is " salt " a substring of " sea salt flax "? Yes! "sea salt flax" contains "salt flax"... wait, " salt " with trailing space: " sea salt flax " — positions: "sea salt" — " salt " appears as substring (the "salt " in "sea salt flax"). Yes, " salt " is found in " sea salt flax " (after "sea"). So `have_supplement salt` returns true incorrectly. That's the plain-salt-vs-sea-salt bug: substring matching matches "salt" inside "sea salt".

Fix: exact element comparison:
```bash
have_supplement() {
  local item
  for item in "${stocked[@]}"; do
    if [ "$item" = "$1" ]; then
      return 0
    fi
  done
  return 1
}
```

6. `horses=$((horses + 1))` — fine. Could be `((horses += 1))` but not needed; shellcheck style might not complain. Actually shellcheck SC2004? No, that's about `$` on vars in arithmetic, which isn't the case here.

Let me check all shellcheck findings by running it.

Also `while read ... done < "$chart"` — after my change, `while IFS=: read -r name scoops feed sup note; do`.

Also check: `read` with 5 fields and note containing spaces: `read name scoops feed sup note` — the last var gets the rest of the line, good. Willow's line: `Willow:1:bran mash::50\50 with hot water` → name=Willow, scoops=1, feed=bran mash, sup=(empty), note=`50\50 with hot water`. With `read -r`, the backslash is preserved: note = `50\50 with hot water`. Good.

Sundance: `Sundance:2:sweet feed:beet pulp:warm the molasses` → note="warm the molasses". Good.

Round A: args "sea salt" flax. Duke: flax stocked → "plus flax". Willow: no sup → note row: "note: 50\50 with hot water". Bramble: sea salt stocked → "plus sea salt". Pip: salt not stocked (with fixed have_supplement) → "plus salt (NOT IN BIN ROOM)", missing+=(salt). Sundance: beet pulp not stocked → "(NOT IN BIN ROOM)", missing+=(beet pulp). Then "short on: salt beet pulp" — with default IFS, `${missing[*]}` joins with space. 

Exit code 3. stderr empty. Good.

Round B: all stocked → exit 0, no "short on" line.

Round C: unreadable chart → warn prints "feedmix: cannot read chart fixture_root/no-such-chart.txt" on stderr, exit 2. With `warn` using `"$@"`, one arg → one line. Good.

Round D: usage → one line. Good.

Also `[ -n "$name" ] || continue` — fine. But SC2262? Not a thing. Let me also consider `read` returning nonzero on last line without newline — chart fixture ends with newline, fine.

Shellcheck findings I expect in the original:
- SC2068 (error): Double quote array expansions — `${row[@]}` in print_row, `$@` in warn.
- SC2162 (info): read without -r.
- Possibly SC2086 etc.

Let me also double-check `have_supplement` regex quoting: SC2076 — "Remove quotes from right-hand side of =~ to match as a regex rather than literally" — that's a warning when the right side is quoted. Actually quoting the RHS makes it literal; shellcheck SC2076 warns "Don't quote rhs of =~, it'll match literally rather than as a regex". So replacing with a loop avoids that.

Also `printf '%s\n' $@` — SC2048/SC2068.

Now the minimal edits:

1. `warn()`: `printf '%s\n' "$@" >&2`
2. `while read` → `while IFS=: read -r` and remove standalone `IFS=:` line.
3. `print_row ${row[@]}` → `print_row "${row[@]}"`
4. `have_supplement` → loop-based exact match.

Also check `[ $status -eq 3 ]` in test — not our file.

One more possible shellcheck nit: `horses=$((horses + 1))` is fine. `local out=""` etc fine.

Also `read name scoops feed sup note` — shellcheck SC2162 fixed with -r. Any complaint about unused vars? No, all used.

What about the shebang and `[ -n "$sup" ]` etc — fine.

Let me run the test first to reproduce, then shellcheck.

messages.content:
Reproduced. The failures map cleanly to four root causes in `feedmix.sh`:

1. **`warn` line 12** — unquoted `$@` word-splits the message, so `printf` prints one word per line (rounds C & D).
2. **`have_supplement` line 41** — `[[ " ${stocked[@]} " =~ " $1 " ]]` is a literal *substring* match, so `salt` matches inside `" sea salt flax "` (the sea-salt bug, round A).
3. **`read` line 51** — no `-r`, so Willow's `50\50` loses its backslash → `5050`.
4. **`print_row ${row[@]}` line 65** — unquoted expansion re-splits the note element on the lingering `IFS=:` → `note |  ...` on the wrong side of the `|`.
5. **The standing `IFS=:` (line 50)** also leaks past the loop and makes `"${missing[*]}"` join the `short on:` list with colons.

Applying the minimal fixes:
messages.reasoning_content:
Reproduced. Now let me map each bug to its root cause:

1. **SC2068 in `warn` (line 12)**: `printf '%s\n' $@` unquoted — each word of the message becomes its own `printf` argument → one word per line (rounds C and D failures). Fix: `"$@"`.

2. **SC2199/SC2076 in `have_supplement` (line 41)**: `[[ " ${stocked[@]} " =~ " $1 " ]]` — with `${stocked[@]}` inside `[[ ]]`, bash implicitly concatenates the array with spaces *without* adding separators around, actually the string becomes " sea salt flax ". Then the quoted `" $1 "` RHS makes it a literal substring match — " salt " IS a substring of " sea salt flax ", so plain `salt` is reported as stocked when only `sea salt` is. That's the Pip/sea-salt bug. Fix: exact-match loop over `"${stocked[@]}"`.

3. **SC2162 (line 51)**: `read` without `-r` eats the backslash in Willow's `50\50` → `5050`. Fix: `read -r`.

4. **SC2068 in `print_row ${row[@]}` (line 65)**: unquoted array expansion re-splits elements on IFS. And note the standing `IFS=:` from line 50 — since IFS is `:`... wait, word splitting uses IFS which is `:`. "note: 50\50 with hot water" — splitting on `:` gives "note" and " 50\50 with hot water" (space preserved since space isn't in IFS). That explains `note |  5050 with hot water` — the note element split on the colon. That's "the sheet splits the note onto the wrong side of the | separators". Fix: quote `"${row[@]}"`.

5. **`short on:` glued with colons**: The persistent `IFS=:` means `"${missing[*]}"` joins with `:`. In the diff we saw "short on: beet pulp" (only one item because salt was wrongly considered stocked). With salt correctly missing, it'd be "salt:beet pulp". Fix: move `IFS=:` into the while-read line (`while IFS=: read -r ...`) so the rest of the script keeps the default IFS.

All five edits are minimal and in the same style. Let me apply them.
Now verify with shellcheck and the acceptance gate.

messages.content:
Done — `bash test_feedmix.sh` prints `ALL CHECKS PASSED` (exit 0), and `shellcheck -S style feedmix.sh` reports zero findings.

**Root causes and fixes** (all in `feedmix.sh`; the chart format, sheet layout, and exit codes are untouched):

1. **`warn` used unquoted `$@`** — `printf '%s\n' $@` got one word per argument, so the usage and unreadable-chart errors printed one word per line. Quoted it (`"$@"`), which also clears SC2068.
2. **`have_supplement` used a substring regex** — `[[ " ${stocked[@]} " =~ " $1 " ]]` matched `" salt "` inside `" sea salt flax "`, so Pip's plain `salt` was reported as stocked. Replaced with an exact-match `for` loop over `"${stocked[@]}"` (also clears SC2199/SC2076).
3. **`read` lacked `-r`** — it ate the backslash in Willow's `50\50 with hot water`, printing `5050`. Now `read -r` (SC2162).
4. **Unquoted `print_row ${row[@]}`** — the standing `IFS=:` made word-splitting cut the note at its colon, landing it on the wrong side of the `|` separators. Quoted as `"${row[@]}"`.
5. **`IFS=:` leaked past the loop** — it also made `"${missing[*]}"` join with colons (`salt:beet pulp`). Moved it into the loop prefix (`while IFS=: read -r ...`) so the rest of the script keeps the default IFS and the `short on:` list is space-joined.

**Proof:** `shellcheck -S style feedmix.sh` exits 0 with no output, and `bash test_feedmix.sh` exits 0 with `ALL CHECKS PASSED` — all four rounds (partly stocked → exit 3, fully stocked → exit 0, unreadable chart → exit 2, usage → exit 2) now match the signed-off sheets byte-for-byte.
messages.reasoning_content: Everything's done. The chart format, sheet layout, and exit codes are unchanged. Summary time.

messages.content:
=== MOONSHINER TASK BOUNDARY ===

Tech-debt ticket on runjobs.sh, our little filing-job runner (reads a tab-separated job list, copies/gzips files out of the drop folder, appends to filing.log). It works, and it has behavior tests that pass — but look at HOW it works: every job is assembled into a command STRING by pasting the filename between hand-placed quote marks, and then the string gets executed. The predictable consequence landed this week: a customer folder contained "client's notes.txt", the apostrophe ate the hand-placed quoting, and the job errored out. Any filename with a quote character in it breaks the runner.

I don't want the quoting patched up with more escaping — I want the string-assembly pattern GONE. Build each job as an argv array and invoke it directly ("${cmd[@]}"-style); the flags column, the -- separator, the output redirections for pack and note all survive that shape just fine. Our CI lint will reject any use of eval in this script, and the new acceptance harness enforces the same with a grep.

Observable behavior must not change AT ALL: same stdout summary, same stderr lines for failed jobs and unknown actions, same exit codes, same file/log outputs — the LEGACY BEHAVIOR half of test_filedrop.sh re-pins all of it, and it passes against the current script today. The REAL-WORLD NAMES half is the new contract: filenames with apostrophes and double quotes go through copy, pack, and note like any other file. bash test_filedrop.sh must pass unmodified; don't touch the test file.

messages.reasoning_content: Let me start by exploring the repository to understand the current state of things.

messages.content: I'll start by reproducing the failure — running the acceptance harness against the current script.
messages.reasoning_content:
Let me understand the current script and the tests.

Current `runjobs.sh`:
- Reads TSV: action, name, flags
- Builds command strings and runs via `eval`
- Actions:
  - `copy`: `cp $flags -- '$src/$name' '$out/$name'`
  - `pack`: `gzip $flags -n -c -- '$src/$name' > '$out/$name.gz'`
  - `note`: `printf '%s\n' 'filed: $name' >> '$out/filing.log'`
- unknown action → stderr "runjobs: unknown action: $action", failed+1, continue
- job failure → stderr "runjobs: job failed: $action $name", failed+1
- Prints "jobs ok: $ok" and "jobs failed: $failed" to stdout
- Exit 0 iff failed == 0, else 1 (via `[ "$failed" -eq 0 ]` as last command)
- Exit 2 on usage/validation errors.

The task: replace string assembly + eval with argv arrays invoked directly, `"${cmd[@]}"` style. The `--` separator, flags column, and output redirections for pack and note survive.

Important subtlety: flags like `-p -f` and `-9` come from a single TSV column. With the current eval, `$flags` is word-split. With an argv array, we need the flags to be split into separate words. So `cmd=(cp $flags -- ...)` with unquoted `$flags` to get word splitting (deliberately). Or use `read -ra flagv <<< "$flags"`. Either works. Since `set -u` is on, an empty flags with unquoted expansion is fine (it's not unset). But note: `set -u` doesn't error on empty-but-set variables. Good.

Another subtlety: with `set -u`, referencing unset positional/vars errors. Fine.

Redirections: `pack` redirects stdout to `$out/$name.gz`, `note` appends to `$out/filing.log`. With argv invocation: `"${cmd[@]}" > "$out/$name.gz"` — redirection happens at invocation site, not inside the array. That preserves behavior.

Error handling: currently `eval "$cmd" 2>/dev/null` — stderr of the command is suppressed. Keep the same: `"${cmd[@]}" 2>/dev/null` for copy, and for pack `"${cmd[@]}" > out.gz 2>/dev/null`, for note `printf ... >> log 2>/dev/null`? Hmm, for note the current command is `printf '%s\n' 'filed: $name' >> '$out/filing.log'`. With eval, stderr of the whole command goes to /dev/null (from the `eval "$cmd" 2>/dev/null`). Keep it consistent.

Let me think about how to structure it. The task says: "Build each job as an argv array and invoke it directly ("${cmd[@]}"-style); the flags column, the -- separator, the output redirections for pack and note all survive that shape just fine."

So keep the same `case` shape building `cmd` as an array:

```bash
case $action in
  copy)
    cmd=(cp $flags -- "$src/$name" "$out/$name")
    redir=
    ;;
  pack)
    cmd=(gzip $flags -n -c -- "$src/$name")
    redir=">$out/$name.gz"   # hmm, redirections can't be stored easily
    ;;
  note)
    cmd=(printf '%s\n' "filed: $name")
    ...
```

Redirections differ per action, so we can handle invocation per action or use a target variable. Options:

Option A: build cmd array in the case, and also set a redirect target variable, then invoke once:

```bash
case $action in
  copy)
    cmd=(cp $flags -- "$src/$name" "$out/$name")
    out_file=
    append=
    ;;
  pack)
    cmd=(gzip $flags -n -c -- "$src/$name")
    out_file=$out/$name.gz
    append=
    ;;
  note)
    cmd=(printf '%s\n' "filed: $name")
    out_file=$out/filing.log
    append=1
    ;;
  *)
    ...
esac
```

Then invoke:

```bash
if [ -z "$out_file" ]; then
  "${cmd[@]}" 2>/dev/null
elif [ -n "$append" ]; then
  "${cmd[@]}" >> "$out_file" 2>/dev/null
else
  "${cmd[@]}" > "$out_file" 2>/dev/null
fi
```

That's a bit clunky. Option B: invoke within each case branch... but then the ok/failed counting logic would be duplicated. Actually we could keep the invocation after the case and just have three redirection modes.

Hmm, simpler: keep the existing structure but with an array and a separate variable for the redirect:

```bash
while IFS=$'\t' read -r action name flags; do
  [ -n "$action" ] || continue
  redirect=
  append=
  case $action in
    copy)
      cmd=(cp $flags -- "$src/$name" "$out/$name")
      ;;
    pack)
      cmd=(gzip $flags -n -c -- "$src/$name")
      redirect=$out/$name.gz
      ;;
    note)
      cmd=(printf '%s\n' "filed: $name")
      redirect=$out/filing.log
      append=yes
      ;;
    *)
      echo "runjobs: unknown action: $action" >&2
      failed=$((failed + 1))
      continue
      ;;
  esac
  if [ -z "$redirect" ]; then
    "${cmd[@]}" 2>/dev/null
    rc=$?
  elif [ -n "$append" ]; then
    "${cmd[@]}" >> "$redirect" 2>/dev/null
    rc=$?
  else
    "${cmd[@]}" > "$redirect" 2>/dev/null
    rc=$?
  fi
  if [ "$rc" -eq 0 ]; then
    ok=$((ok + 1))
  else
    echo "runjobs: job failed: $action $name" >&2
    failed=$((failed + 1))
  fi
done < "$jobs"
```

Careful with `set -u`: `cmd` and variables referenced must be set. `redirect` and `append` are set at loop top. Good.

Wait — one subtlety about `set -u` and empty arrays: `"${cmd[@]}"` with an empty array under `set -u` errors in bash < 4.4. But cmd always has ≥1 element here. Fine.

Another subtlety: unquoted `$flags` in the array assignment. `cmd=(cp $flags -- ...)` — word splitting applies to unquoted expansion, and glob expansion too. If flags contained a `*`, globbing could happen. Legacy eval also globbed (actually eval would re-glob). Behavior parity is fine. But could I use `read -ra flagv <<< "$flags"` then `cmd=(cp "${flagv[@]}" ...)`? That avoids globbing. Under `set -u`, `"${flagv[@]}"` with empty array on old bash (4.3 and earlier) fails. Bash version on this machine likely 5.x. Let me check bash --version. Simpler to keep unquoted `$flags` which matches the "flags column survives that shape" hint — the task literally says flags survive "${cmd[@]}" shape, implying `cmd=(cp $flags -- ...)`.

Hmm, but with `set -u` and unquoted empty `$flags`... `flags` is set by `read`, even if empty (set to empty string). With `set -u`, expanding an empty set variable is fine. Word splitting of empty unquoted expansion yields zero words. 

Edge: IFS is set to tab within the `while read` line only? Actually `IFS=$'\t' read -r ...` sets IFS only for the read command's environment... no — `IFS=... read` assigns IFS for the duration of `read` only (it's a prefix assignment, which for a builtin like read affects the builtin's execution and, for special builtins, persists... `read` is not a special builtin per POSIX; in bash, prefix assignments before non-special builtins do NOT persist). So IFS inside the loop body is the default space-tab-newline. So unquoted `$flags` splits on spaces too — "−p -f" splits into two words. Good, matches eval behavior.

But wait — careful: filename `$name` with spaces is quoted: `"$src/$name"`. Good.

Another consideration: legacy behavior for a failing `copy` job when flags are empty etc. `cp -- src dst 2>/dev/null` — same stderr suppression, same exit code semantics.

One more parity detail: the current script runs `eval "$cmd" 2>/dev/null; then ...` — the `if` checks exit status. In my rewrite I capture rc after the command. Careful: `rc=$?` must come right after. Using `if "${cmd[@]}" ...; then` directly is cleaner:

```bash
if [ -z "$redirect" ]; then
  if "${cmd[@]}" 2>/dev/null; then ... 
```

That triples the ok/failed logic. Better to keep single counting block with rc capture. Or use a function. Let me write:

```bash
if [ -n "$redirect" ]; then
  if [ -n "$append" ]; then
    "${cmd[@]}" >> "$redirect" 2>/dev/null
  else
    "${cmd[@]}" > "$redirect" 2>/dev/null
  fi
else
  "${cmd[@]}" 2>/dev/null
fi
rc=$?
```

Hmm, `rc=$?` after the if statement gets the if's exit status, which is the last command executed inside — that works since if with no else returns 0 if no condition true... wait: if `[ -n "$redirect" ]` is false and there's an else branch, the else runs and the if's status is the else's status. Fine, all branches covered. Actually, careful: `if cmd1; then cmd2; fi` exit status is cmd2's if cmd1 true; if cmd1 false and no else, status is 0. We have else, so always the executed branch's status. Then `rc=$?` captures it. Good.

Alternatively simplest and readable:

```bash
copy)  cmd=(cp $flags -- "$src/$name" "$out/$name") ;;
pack)  cmd=(gzip $flags -n -c -- "$src/$name") ;;
note)  cmd=(printf '%s\n' "filed: $name") ;;
```

and then

```bash
case $action in
  copy) "${cmd[@]}" 2>/dev/null ;;
  pack) "${cmd[@]}" > "$out/$name.gz" 2>/dev/null ;;
  note) "${cmd[@]}" >> "$out/filing.log" 2>/dev/null ;;
esac
rc=$?
```

Hmm, that repeats $action case. Both fine. Let me think about what's cleanest while preserving style. The existing style uses full `if [ ... ]` and simple constructs, `echo`, not printf-heavy. I'll go with the redirect variable approach — it keeps one invocation site, matching the original single `eval` site. Actually the task statement says "the output redirections for pack and note all survive that shape just fine", hinting redirection at invocation site.

One more consideration: note action and `set -u` with `printf '%s\n' "filed: $name"` — fine.

What about names with backslashes? `read -r` handles them raw; legacy eval had issues but not our problem; array handles them better anyway.

What about the `note` case for filename containing `%`? printf format is fixed '%s\n', argument "filed: $name". Fine.

What about empty flags under `set -u`? As discussed, fine.

Now — wait, one more parity check on flags splitting: in the eval version, `$flags` inside the string is subject to word splitting AND globbing at eval time. In array version, `cmd=(cp $flags -- ...)` does word splitting + globbing at assignment time. Same effective behavior.

Another parity check: in legacy, `cmd="cp $flags -- ..."` — when flags empty, string is "cp  -- ..." and eval's word splitting removes empties. Array: `cmd=(cp -- ...)`. Same.

Trailing empty flags column: test TSV lines like `copy\tplain.txt\t\n` — read with IFS=tab gets flags=''. Good.

What about `read` when the last line lacks trailing newline... not tested; keep behavior.

Also `[ -n "$action" ] || continue` — skip blank lines. Keep.

Also note the exit status: `[ "$failed" -eq 0 ]` as final command. Keep.

Now let me also double-check the eval-grep in the test: `(^|[^[:alnum:]_])eval([^[:alnum:]_]|$)` — my script must not contain the word "eval" anywhere, including comments. I'll avoid it in comments. Words like "evaluate" would NOT match because `eval` followed by `u` is alnum, so `([^[:alnum:]_]|$)` fails — "evaluate" is safe, but I'll just avoid it entirely to be safe. Note my redirection variable named... avoid "eval". Use `redirect` / `append`. Good.

Let me now write the new runjobs.sh:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# runjobs.sh -- apply the day's filing jobs to the drop folder.
#
# Job list is tab-separated: <action> <name> <flags>
#   copy  copy <src>/<name> to <out>/<name>          (flags passed to cp)
#   pack  gzip <src>/<name> to <out>/<name>.gz       (flags passed to gzip)
#   note  append "filed: <name>" to <out>/filing.log
set -u
LC_ALL=C
export LC_ALL

if [ $# -ne 3 ]; then
  echo "usage: runjobs.sh <jobs.tsv> <src-dir> <out-dir>" >&2
  exit 2
fi

jobs=$1
src=$2
out=$3

if [ ! -f "$jobs" ]; then
  echo "runjobs: no such job list: $jobs" >&2
  exit 2
fi
if [ ! -d "$src" ]; then
  echo "runjobs: no such source dir: $src" >&2
  exit 2
fi
mkdir -p "$out"

ok=0
failed=0
while IFS=$'\t' read -r action name flags; do
  [ -n "$action" ] || continue
  redirect=
  append=
  case $action in
    copy)
      cmd=(cp $flags -- "$src/$name" "$out/$name")
      ;;
    pack)
      cmd=(gzip $flags -n -c -- "$src/$name")
      redirect=$out/$name.gz
      ;;
    note)
      cmd=(printf '%s\n' "filed: $name")
      redirect=$out/filing.log
      append=yes
      ;;
    *)
      echo "runjobs: unknown action: $action" >&2
      failed=$((failed + 1))
      continue
      ;;
  esac
  if [ -z "$redirect" ]; then
    "${cmd[@]}" 2>/dev/null
  elif [ -n "$append" ]; then
    "${cmd[@]}" >> "$redirect" 2>/dev/null
  else
    "${cmd[@]}" > "$redirect" 2>/dev/null
  fi
  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    ok=$((ok + 1))
  else
    echo "runjobs: job failed: $action $name" >&2
    failed=$((failed + 1))
  fi
done < "$jobs"

echo "jobs ok: $ok"
echo "jobs failed: $failed"
[ "$failed" -eq 0 ]
```

Wait: `if [ $? -eq 0 ]` right after the if-redirect block — `$?` is the exit status of the whole if statement, which is the last-run command. That works. But it's a little subtle; more idiomatic:

```bash
if [ -z "$redirect" ]; then
  "${cmd[@]}" 2>/dev/null
elif ...
fi
rc=$?
if [ "$rc" -eq 0 ]; then ...
```

Hmm wait, does `if...fi` followed by `rc=$?` capture correctly? Yes, `$?` after an if statement is the exit status of the if (last command executed in a branch). Since every branch ends with the command invocation, rc is the command's status. 

But hold on, subtle case: if `[ -z "$redirect" ]` itself... if the test command `[` fails (non-empty redirect), we move to elif — the status is from the branch that ran. All branches end with the invocation. Fine.

Alternatively avoid the branching entirely using an indirect approach: store redirect as an array too? Like `post=('>' file)`? Can't apply redirection from array. Keep branching.

Actually, could simplify: default redirect to /dev/stdout? No — pack writes gzip to stdout and we redirect to file; note appends. Could set `redirect=/dev/stdout` with truncate for copy? cp doesn't write stdout... it would do `"${cmd[@]}" > /dev/stdout 2>/dev/null` which is equivalent to no redirect. Eh, that's cute but /dev/stdout redirection is not identical in spirit. Keep the branchy version, it's clear.

Hmm, one more thought on flags word splitting and `set -u`: none.

messages.content:
Done. Summary:

**Root cause:** Each job was assembled as a command *string* by pasting `$name` between hand-placed single quotes (`cmd="cp $flags -- '$src/$name' ..."`) and executed via `eval "$cmd"`. Any quote character in a filename (e.g. `client's notes.txt`) terminated that hand-placed quoting, the string parsed as garbage, and the job errored out.

**Change (runjobs.sh only, test file untouched):** The string assembly and `eval` are gone. Each action now builds an argv array — `cmd=(cp $flags -- "$src/$name" "$out/$name")`, `cmd=(gzip $flags -n -c -- "$src/$name")`, `cmd=(printf '%s\n' "filed: $name")` — invoked directly as `"${cmd[@]}"`. The flags column is deliberately left unquoted so it still word-splits into separate argv words (commented in the script); the `--` separator and the per-action redirections survive at the invocation site (`> "$redirect"` for pack, `>>` for note), and job stderr is still silenced with `2>/dev/null` exactly as before. All surrounding logic — quiet stderr on success, `runjobs: job failed:` / `runjobs: unknown action:` lines, ok/failed counts, exit codes 0/1/2 — is unchanged.

**Proof:**
- `bash test_filedrop.sh` (unmodified): **ok - 20 checks passed**, exit 0 — both the LEGACY BEHAVIOR half (byte-pinned stdout/stderr/exit codes, copied/gzipped/log outputs) and the REAL-WORLD NAMES half (apostrophe and double-quote names through copy/pack/note, plus the no-`eval` grep gate).
- Before the fix the same harness failed 7/20, all in the quoted-names half — reproduced first.
- `bash -n` clean; no `eval` token remains.
- Manual parity probe against `git show HEAD:runjobs.sh`: mixed-quote name `odd'mix' "q" & $.txt` now copies (legacy failed it — the intended fix), while the failed-`pack` artifact (empty `.gz`), stderr format, and exit code 1 are byte-identical to legacy. Probe files cleaned up; only `runjobs.sh` is modified.

messages.content:
=== MOONSHINER TASK BOUNDARY ===

The nightly stock export lands in ops as bare CSV and everyone squints at it in a terminal or pastes it into chat where it turns to soup. I want a pretty-printer: `table.sh` in the repo root, pure bash, that renders one of these files as an aligned fixed-width table.

About the input, so nobody gold-plates this: our exporter NEVER quotes. A comma is always a field separator, there is no escaping of any kind, and RFC-4180 quoted fields are explicitly OUT of scope — real CSV parsing lives elsewhere in the stack, this is a renderer. Files are ASCII with LF line endings. Splitting is dumb on purpose: N commas means N+1 fields, so `a,b,` is three fields with the third one empty. Cells keep their spaces verbatim — no trimming, and spaces count toward width.

Usage: `table.sh <file.csv> [maxwidth]`. maxwidth caps every column and defaults to 16; it must be a decimal integer >= 5, anything else gets `table.sh: maxwidth must be an integer >= 5` on stderr and exit 64 (validate it before touching the file). No arguments at all: `usage: table.sh <file.csv> [maxwidth]` on stderr, exit 64. Missing or unreadable file: `table.sh: cannot read: <arg>` on stderr, exit 66.

Rendering rules, and I mean them precisely:
- The first line is the header and fixes the column count C. Validate before you print anything: every data line must have exactly C fields; on the FIRST offender print `table.sh: line <N>: expected <C> fields, got <K>` to stderr (N is the 1-based physical line number) and exit 65 with NOTHING on stdout. A zero-line file: `table.sh: empty input` to stderr, exit 65.
- A column is numeric iff it has at least one data row AND every one of its data cells matches the ERE `^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$`. The header cell never counts, and an empty cell, `1e3`, ` 7`, `+5` all disqualify. Numeric columns right-align; everything else left-aligns; the header cell follows its column's alignment.
- Column width W = min(maxwidth, longest cell in the column, header included). Any cell longer than W renders as its first W-3 characters plus `...` — that is three ASCII dots, NOT a Unicode ellipsis; our terminals and diffs stay ASCII. Numeric detection looks at ORIGINAL cell values; truncation is display-only.
- Render: pad every cell to exactly W (left-pad when right-aligned, right-pad when left-aligned), join cells with ` | ` (space pipe space), then strip trailing spaces off each finished line — no output line ever ends in a space. After the header print a separator line: W dashes per column, columns joined with `-+-`. Data rows follow in input order. On success stderr stays empty and the exit code is 0. A header-only file renders header + separator and exits 0.
- Pure bash. Width math with `${#cell}`, padding with printf, reading with builtins. No awk, no column(1), no sed, no external commands for any of the rendering. (Pure bash also keeps it happy on the macOS boxes.)

Spec by example — this input, default maxwidth:

item,qty,unit price,notes
widget,12,3.50,fast mover
long-tail adapter cable spool,3,17.25,check reorder threshold soon
gizmo,-4,0.99,

must render byte-for-byte as:

item             | qty | unit price | notes
-----------------+-----+------------+-----------------
widget           |  12 |       3.50 | fast mover
long-tail ada... |   3 |      17.25 | check reorder...
gizmo            |  -4 |       0.99 |

(Note qty and unit price right-align because every data cell is numeric; notes is text; the long cells truncate to thirteen characters plus the three dots; the last line ends right after the final `|` because the empty notes cell right-stripped away.)

Acceptance: `bash test_table.sh` from the workspace root must pass unmodified — do not touch the test file.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# table.sh -- render a bare CSV file as an aligned fixed-width table.
# Usage: table.sh <file.csv> [maxwidth]

if (($# == 0)); then
  printf 'usage: table.sh <file.csv> [maxwidth]\n' >&2
  exit 64
fi

file=$1
maxw=16
if (($# >= 2)); then
  if [[ ! $2 =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]] || ((10#$2 < 5)); then
    printf 'table.sh: maxwidth must be an integer >= 5\n' >&2
    exit 64
  fi
  maxw=$((10#$2))
fi

if [[ -d $file || ! -r $file ]]; then
  printf 'table.sh: cannot read: %s\n' "$file" >&2
  exit 66
fi

# split <line> -> global array CELL (N commas -> N+1 fields, empties kept)
split() {
  local rest=$1
  CELL=()
  while [[ $rest == *,* ]]; do
    CELL+=("${rest%%,*}")
    rest=${rest#*,}
  done
  CELL+=("$rest")
}

# slurp every line first: nothing may be printed before validation passes
lines=()
nlines=0
while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n $line ]]; do
  lines+=("$line")
  ((++nlines))
done < "$file"

if ((nlines == 0)); then
  printf 'table.sh: empty input\n' >&2
  exit 65
fi

split "${lines[0]}"
header=("${CELL[@]}")
C=${#header[@]}

# per-column state
width=()      # longest ORIGINAL cell, header included
numeric=()    # 1 until a data cell disqualifies the column
for ((c = 0; c < C; ++c)); do
  width[c]=${#header[c]}
  numeric[c]=1
done

for ((i = 1; i < nlines; ++i)); do
  split "${lines[i]}"
  if ((${#CELL[@]} != C)); then
    printf 'table.sh: line %d: expected %d fields, got %d\n' \
      "$((i + 1))" "$C" "${#CELL[@]}" >&2
    exit 65
  fi
  for ((c = 0; c < C; ++c)); do
    ((${#CELL[c]} > width[c])) && width[c]=${#CELL[c]}
    [[ ${CELL[c]} =~ ^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$ ]] || numeric[c]=0
  done
done

# a column is numeric only if it has at least one data row
if ((nlines == 1)); then
  for ((c = 0; c < C; ++c)); do numeric[c]=0; done
fi

# final display width per column
W=()
for ((c = 0; c < C; ++c)); do
  ((width[c] > maxw)) && W[c]=$maxw || W[c]=${width[c]}
done
```

Done. `bash test_table.sh` passes **all 39 checks unmodified**, and the spec-by-example output diffs byte-for-byte identical to the task description.

**What I built** — `table.sh` in the repo root, pure bash (only `printf`, `read`, `[[ ]]`, `(( ))`, arrays, and parameter expansion; the grep hits above are just the word "column" in comments).

**The one real trap:** `IFS=, read -ra` silently drops trailing empty fields (`a,b,` yields 2 elements, not 3), which would have broken both field-count validation and the `x | y |` / `a | b |` rendering. So splitting is a small manual loop (`split()`) that takes everything before the first comma repeatedly, guaranteeing N commas → N+1 fields with spaces kept verbatim.

**How it works, in spec order:**
1. **Validation before any output:** no args → usage/64; maxwidth checked next (must match `^[0-9]+$` and be ≥ 5, via `10#$2` so `08` doesn't explode as octal) *before* the file is even looked at → 64; then `-r`/`-d` check → `cannot read`/66. The whole file is slurped into an array first, so the empty-file check (65) and the first field-count offender (65, physical 1-based line number) fire with zero bytes on stdout.
2. **Stats pass:** per-column longest *original* cell (header included) and a numeric flag cleared by any data cell failing `^-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$` (so ` 7`, `1e3`, `+5`, and empty cells all disqualify); header-only files force every column non-numeric/left-aligned.
3. **Rendering:** `W = min(maxw, longest)`; over-long cells (headers included) render as `${cell:0:W-3}...`; `printf '%*s'`/`'%-*s'` pads to exactly W with right-align for numeric columns; cells joined ` | `, trailing spaces stripped per line; separator is W dashes per column joined with `-+-` (its flanking dashes merge visually, which is why `a,b,c` with widths 2/3/1 yields `---+-----+--`).

**Extra probes beyond the harness** (all correct): missing final newline still parses the last line; bad maxwidth beats missing file (64 precedence); `4`, `0`, `-5`, `5.5`, ` 5`, `+5`, empty, `wide` all rejected while `008` is accepted; all-empty data column stays left-aligned; `-0.5` numeric vs `.` not; a mid-file empty line reports the right physical line number.

=== MOONSHINER TASK BOUNDARY ===

Two cron entries on the same runner occasionally start the same reindex job a minute apart and they trample each other's temp tables. I want a tiny guard wrapper: `withlock.sh`, repo root, bash 5, `#!/usr/bin/env bash`. Our runners are Linux with util-linux flock(1) installed — using it is the point; don't hand-roll the mutual exclusion itself. Everything else is plain shell.

Call: `bash withlock.sh <lockfile> <command> [args...]`. Fewer than two arguments -> `usage: withlock.sh <lockfile> <command> [args...]` on stderr, exit 64.

Our lock protocol, which the wrapper must implement exactly:

- The lockfile is both the flock target and the holder record: while a run holds the lock, the file's first line is that run's PID; when free, the file is empty (zero bytes). Create it if it doesn't exist. NEVER open it with a truncating redirect before you hold the lock — append-mode open, then `flock -n` on that descriptor.
- flock says busy -> someone is actively holding it. Append one line to `<lockfile>.queue` — the command words joined by single spaces — print `withlock.sh: busy: <lockfile>` to stderr, exit 75. The queue file is an append-only history; the wrapper never truncates or rewrites it (ops greps it to see what got skipped overnight).
- flock acquired -> look at the recorded PID before you trust the lock:
  - file empty: it's yours.
  - records a process that no longer exists (kill -0 fails): that's a leftover from an interrupted run — print `withlock.sh: clearing leftover lock: <lockfile>` to stderr, then proceed to take it.
  - records a process that IS alive even though the flock was free (a holder started by the old pre-flock tooling): not yours — same busy path as above, queue + message + 75. Leave the file's contents exactly as they were.
- Taking it: write your own PID as the file's only line, hold the flock for the whole run, run the command with its stdout/stderr passed straight through, and exit with the COMMAND's exit code, zero or not. On the way out — success or command failure alike — empty the lockfile again so the next caller finds it free. PIDs live only inside the lockfile; they never appear in the wrapper's own output.

The acceptance harness scripts a real contention scene (a background holder pinned open by a flag file, two turned-away callers, release, retry) plus the leftover-lock and live-legacy-holder cases. `bash test_flockq.sh` from the workspace root must pass unmodified — don't touch the test file.

messages.content:
=== MOONSHINER TASK BOUNDARY ===

I run facilities inventory for two floors and our 'system' is a spreadsheet that eats leading spaces and reorders itself on every export. The label scanners already give me an honest feed file; I just need it summarized. One bash script, `inv.sh`, in the repo root — the boxes have bash 5, and I don't want awk or python dragged into this. Do the counting the bash way: associative arrays (declare -A), IFS-safe reads, quoted expansions everywhere. The ONLY external tool you get is sort(1) for the final ordering, run as `LC_ALL=C sort`. It also has to behave on the macOS laptops, so nothing GNU-only anywhere.

The feed is one scan per line: `<asset><TAB><location>`, exactly one tab. Real lines from last night, so you see what the names are like:

label printer	front desk
power strip, 8-way	rack A7
-r drive caddy	rack B2

The asset names are whatever is printed on the label: commas, doubled spaces, leading spaces, leading dashes, brackets like [cat6], stars, the occasional café glyph. Treat both fields as opaque bytes — no trimming, used verbatim — and mind your quoting, because half of those look like globs to a careless script. Blank lines and lines whose first character is `#` are padding/comments: skip silently. Anything else that isn't exactly asset-tab-location with both sides non-empty (no tab at all, two tabs, empty asset, empty location) is malformed: print `inv.sh: line <N>: malformed, skipped` to stderr — N is the physical line number counting every line from 1, comments and blanks included — and keep going. Warnings never change the exit code.

The report goes to stdout, byte-exact, three sections always present even when they are empty:

BY-LOCATION
<location><TAB><distinct asset names seen there><TAB><scans there>
REPEATED
<asset><TAB><location><TAB><count>          (only pairs scanned 2+ times)
TOTALS
locations<TAB><number of distinct locations>
assets<TAB><distinct asset names across the whole feed>
scans<TAB><total accepted data lines>
repeated<TAB><number of pairs with count >= 2>

Rows inside BY-LOCATION and inside REPEATED are sorted in plain LC_ALL=C byte order of the whole row line — I diff these reports day to day, so the order has to be identical run to run, no hash-order leaking through.

Exit codes: 0 on success (malformed-line warnings included); called with no argument -> `usage: inv.sh <feed-file>` on stderr, exit 64; the file is missing or unreadable -> `inv.sh: cannot read: <arg>` on stderr, exit 66.

Now write the script. Style: clean bash, comments where useful. Shebang `#!/usr/bin/env bash` — though invoked via `bash inv.sh`, shebang is good practice.

Full script:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# inv.sh -- summarize a scanner feed of <asset><TAB><location> lines.
#
# Pure bash (associative arrays, IFS-safe reads); the only external
# command used is LC_ALL=C sort for the final row ordering.

# ---- arguments --------------------------------------------------------------

if (( $# != 1 )); then
  printf 'usage: inv.sh <feed-file>\n' >&2
  exit 64
fi

feed=$1
if [[ -d $feed || ! -r $feed ]]; then
  printf 'inv.sh: cannot read: %s\n' "$feed" >&2
  exit 66
fi

# ---- pass 1: count ----------------------------------------------------------

declare -A loc_scans=()      # location            -> scans seen there
declare -A loc_assets=()     # location            -> distinct asset names
declare -A loc_asset_seen=() # location<TAB>asset  -> 1 (dedup set)
declare -A asset_seen=()     # asset               -> 1 (dedup set)
declare -A pair_count=()     # asset<TAB>location  -> scans of that pair

scans=0
lineno=0

while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n $line ]]; do
  lineno=$((lineno + 1))

  # padding: blank lines and comments
  [[ -z $line ]] && continue
  [[ $line == '#'* ]] && continue

  # must be exactly asset<TAB>location, both sides non-empty
  if [[ $line != *$'\t'* || $line == *$'\t'*$'\t'* ]]; then
    printf 'inv.sh: line %d: malformed, skipped\n' "$lineno" >&2
    continue
  fi
  asset=${line%%$'\t'*}
  location=${line#*$'\t'}
  if [[ -z $asset || -z $location ]]; then
    printf 'inv.sh: line %d: malformed, skipped\n' "$lineno" >&2
    continue
  fi

  scans=$((scans + 1))
  loc_scans[$location]=$(( ${loc_scans[$location]:-0} + 1 ))

  la_key=$location$'\t'$asset
  if [[ -z ${loc_asset_seen[$la_key]+seen} ]]; then
    loc_asset_seen[$la_key]=1
    loc_assets[$location]=$(( ${loc_assets[$location]:-0} + 1 ))
  fi

  asset_seen[$asset]=1

  pair_key=$asset$'\t'$location
  pair_count[$pair_key]=$(( ${pair_count[$pair_key]:-0} + 1 ))
done < "$feed"

# ---- report -----------------------------------------------------------------

printf 'BY-LOCATION\n'
loc_rows=()
for location in "${!loc_scans[@]}"; do
  loc_rows+=( "$location"$'\t'"${loc_assets[$location]}"$'\t'"${loc_scans[$location]}" )
done
if (( ${#loc_rows[@]} > 0 )); then
  printf '%s\n' "${loc_rows[@]}" | LC_ALL=C sort
fi

printf 'REPEATED\n'
rep_rows=()
for pair_key in "${!pair_count[@]}"; do
  count=${pair_count[$pair_key]}
  if (( count >= 2 )); then
    rep_rows+=( "$pair_key"$'\t'"$count" )
  fi
done
if (( ${#rep_rows[@]} > 0 )); then
  printf '%s\n' "${rep_rows[@]}" | LC_ALL=C sort
fi

printf 'TOTALS\n'
printf 'locations\t%d\n' "${#loc_scans[@]}"
printf 'assets\t%d\n' "${#asset_seen[@]}"
printf 'scans\t%d\n' "$scans"
printf 'repeated\t%d\n' "${#rep_rows[@]}"

exit 0
```

messages.content:
Done. `bash test_inv.sh` passes unmodified: **all checks passed (19 checks)**, exit 0, and the workspace contains only `inv.sh` and the untouched test file.

**What was built** — `inv.sh`, a pure-bash summarizer (no awk/python; the only external command is `LC_ALL=C sort`, invoked exactly that way at two sites — fully portable to macOS/BSD):

- **Reading**: whole lines via `IFS= read -r line || [[ -n $line ]]` — preserves leading/doubled spaces and backslashes verbatim, and accepts a final line with no trailing newline. Deliberately *not* `IFS=$'\t' read`: tab is IFS-whitespace, which would collapse `\t\t`, strip a leading tab, and make empty fields undetectable.
- **Validation**: parameter-expansion patterns require exactly one tab (`!= *$'\t'*` or `== *$'\t'*$'\t'*` → malformed), then `asset=${line%%$'\t'*}` / `location=${line#*$'\t'}` with both sides required non-empty. Warnings carry the physical line number (comments/blanks counted) and never affect the exit code.
- **Counting** (`declare -A`): per-location scans, per-location distinct assets (via a dedup set), global asset set, and asset×location pair counts. TAB itself is the composite-key separator — fields provably contain no tab, so keys can't collide.
- **Report**: rows built as plain strings, each section's rows piped through `LC_ALL=C sort` (whole-line byte order, no hash-order leakage). The sort calls are count-guarded so an empty section emits nothing — no stray blank line from `printf` with zero args.
- **Exit codes**: 64 with `usage: inv.sh <feed-file>` on stderr for wrong arg count; 66 with `inv.sh: cannot read: <arg>` for missing/unreadable/directory; 0 otherwise.

**Verification beyond the gate** (probes cleaned up afterwards): byte-identical output across 20 consecutive runs (determinism), a feed whose fields are literal `*`, `? ,`, `[` stays byte-exact even with glob-matching decoy files (`c`, `old-spare-fan`) in the cwd, empty file → zeroed TOTALS, final line without newline counted, and `bash -n` clean on bash 5.3.
messages.reasoning_content:
Everything passes. Workspace is clean (only inv.sh + test_inv.sh, probe dirs removed). Summarize:

- Root cause: there was no inv.sh; built it.
- Key design decisions worth reporting:
  - Tab is IFS-whitespace, so `IFS=$'\t' read` would collapse empty fields and lose leading-tab info — instead read whole lines with `IFS= read -r` and split via parameter expansion after validating exactly one tab.
  - Tab as composite-key separator (fields provably can't contain tabs).
  - `LC_ALL=C sort` on whole row lines; sort call guarded so empty sections don't get a stray blank line from printf.
  - Physical line numbers counted for every line including comments/blanks.
- Proof: gate passes (19 checks), plus edge probes (no trailing newline, empty file, missing file rc=66, usage rc=64, 20-run determinism, glob-hostile feeds with decoy files in cwd).

messages.content:
=== MOONSHINER TASK BOUNDARY ===

Our maintenance batches run as a flat list of shell one-liners and we currently run them serially; the nightly window is getting tight. I tried `xargs -P` and hated it: no per-job log, exit codes vanish, and the batch 'succeeds' even when half the jobs failed. Build me `pool.sh` (repo root, bash 5, `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, plain shell + coreutils, nothing GNU-only) — a bounded parallel runner driven by `wait -n`, not a hand-rolled polling loop.

Call: `bash pool.sh <manifest> <max-workers>`.

Manifest format, one job per line: `<name><TAB><command>`. Name is a simple token (letters, digits, underscore, dash). Command is EVERYTHING after the first tab, run via `bash -c` from the pool's working directory — commands may themselves contain tabs, quotes, whatever. Blank lines and lines starting with `#` are skipped.

Validate the whole manifest BEFORE running anything: for each bad line print `pool.sh: line <N>: malformed` (physical line numbers, blanks and comments counted) — that covers missing tab, empty name, empty command, bad name characters; a reused name gets `pool.sh: line <N>: duplicate job name: <name>`. Messages in line order. If anything was bad: exit 65 and run NOTHING — no logs dir, no results file, no side effects from any command.

Execution: start jobs in manifest order, never more than <max-workers> running at once — free a slot with `wait -n` as jobs finish. Each job's stdout AND stderr go to `logs/<name>.log` (create `logs/`, truncate any old log; send stderr into the same descriptor so the job's own write order is preserved — do not split streams into two files). Every finished job appends one line `<name><TAB><exit-status>` to `results.txt` (truncate it fresh at batch start; completion order is fine, the auditors sort it anyway). When all jobs are done print exactly `pool: <total> jobs, <failed> failed` on stdout; exit 0 if every job exited 0, else 1. An empty manifest (only comments/blanks) is legal: logs/ and an empty results.txt still get created, summary says 0 jobs, exit 0.

Errors: wrong argument count, or a worker count that isn't an integer >= 1 -> `usage: pool.sh <manifest> <max-workers>` on stderr, exit 64. Manifest missing/unreadable -> `pool.sh: cannot read: <arg>` on stderr, exit 66.

The acceptance test drives a gated batch that forces jobs to overlap and measures the concurrency ceiling with marker files, so a runner that secretly serializes — or one that ignores the bound — both fail. `bash test_jobpool.sh` from the workspace root must pass unmodified; don't touch the test file.

```bash
( bash -c "$cmd" > "logs/$name.log" 2>&1; printf '%s\t%s\n' "$name" "$?" >> results.txt ) &
```
But then results.txt is appended by background jobs concurrently — for short lines (< PIPE_BUF, 4096 bytes) appends with O_APPEND are atomic-ish. Actually `>>` redirection uses O_APPEND and printf to a file... each printf is a single write for short strings, so lines won't interleave in practice. But the spec says "Every finished job appends one line" — completion order fine. However, the summary count of failed jobs needs the statuses in the parent. The parent can count failures from results.txt at the end, or better: capture statuses via `wait -n`.

Hmm, but a cleaner way that satisfies "driven by wait -n": use `wait -n -p` if bash ≥ 5.1. Let me check the bash version on this machine.

Also an important subtlety: how do we associate the exit status with the name? With `wait -n -p var`, we get the pid, look up name in assoc array, get status from `$?`. 

If bash is 5.0 only: fallback — after `wait -n` (without args, waits for any child and returns its status... wait, does plain `wait -n` without arguments return the status of the job? Yes: "If ID is not given, wait waits for all currently active child processes, and the return status is zero." With -n: "waits for any single job to terminate and returns its exit status". And without -p, we don't know which pid. Hmm.

One robust trick for bash 5.0: launch each job wrapped so the exit status is communicated:
```bash

pid=$!
```
Then `wait -n` reaps one job, status = $?. To find which pid, we can compare the set: keep list of "live" pids; after wait -n, loop over the live pids and use `kill -0 $pid 2>/dev/null`. The reaped one will fail kill -0 (already reaped by wait). Zombies not yet reaped pass kill -0. So the first pid in our list that fails kill -0 is (one of) the reaped one(s). Since wait -n reaped exactly one job (the one whose status it returned), exactly one pid in our list should fail kill -0... unless a previously-finished job was never reaped — but we reap one per wait -n call, and each finish triggers exactly one wait -n call, so the number of reaped equals finished. So the reaped one = the one that fails kill -0. There could be ambiguity if the same call reaped one but another one also... no: reaped children fail kill -0; only wait reaps; we call wait -n once per loop iteration and immediately scan; previously scanned/reaped pids are removed from our list. So in each iteration, after wait -n, exactly one pid in the current live list has been reaped by that call (the others, even if exited, are zombies). But wait — pids reaped in previous iterations were already removed from the live list. So scanning finds exactly one. That works but is O(n²) and hacky. 

Since the task says "bash 5", and bash 5.1's `wait -n -p` is cleanest, let me check the version. Most modern systems have bash 5.1 or 5.2. Let me check.

Design:


```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# pool.sh -- bounded parallel runner for tab-separated job manifests.
#
#   bash pool.sh <manifest> <max-workers>
#
# Manifest: one job per line, "<name>\t<command>".  Blank lines and lines
# starting with '#' are ignored.  The whole manifest is validated before
# anything runs.  Each job runs via bash -c with stdout+stderr merged into
# logs/<name>.log; finished jobs append "<name>\t<status>" to results.txt.

usage() {
  printf 'usage: pool.sh <manifest> <max-workers>\n' >&2
  exit 64
}

[[ $# -eq 2 ]] || usage
manifest=$1
max_workers=$2

# worker count must be a positive integer
[[ $max_workers =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]] || usage
(( 10#$max_workers >= 1 )) || usage
```

```bash
  if [[ -v "seen[$name]" ]]; then

  fi
  seen[$name]=1

done < "$manifest"
```

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# pool.sh -- bounded parallel runner for tab-separated job manifests.
#
# Usage: bash pool.sh <manifest> <max-workers>
#
# Manifest: one job per line, "<name><TAB><command>".  Blank lines and
# lines whose first character is '#' are ignored.  The command is run via
# "bash -c" from the pool's working directory and may contain anything
# (including further tabs).
#
# The manifest is validated in full before anything runs; on any error the
# batch is refused (exit 65) with no side effects.  Jobs run with at most
# <max-workers> concurrent processes; slots are freed with "wait -n" as
# jobs finish.  Each job's stdout and stderr are merged into
# logs/<name>.log, and each finished job appends "<name><TAB><status>" to
# results.txt.  Exit status is 0 when every job succeeded, else 1.

usage() {
  printf 'usage: pool.sh <manifest> <max-workers>\n' >&2
  exit 64
}

# ---- arguments ---------------------------------------------------------------

[[ $# -eq 2 ]] || usage
manifest=$1
[[ $2 =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]] || usage
limit=$((10#$2))
(( limit >= 1 )) || usage

if [[ ! -r $manifest ]]; then
  printf 'pool.sh: cannot read: %s\n' "$manifest" >&2
  exit 66
fi

# ---- manifest validation (whole file, before anything runs) ------------------

tab=$'\t'
lineno=0
bad=0
declare -A seen=()
names=()
cmds=()

while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n $line ]]; do
  lineno=$((lineno + 1))
  [[ -z $line ]] && continue            # blank
  [[ $line == '#'* ]] && continue       # comment
  if [[ $line != *"$tab"* || ${line%%"$tab"*} = '' ]] ...
```

```bash
while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n $line ]]; do
  lineno=$((lineno + 1))
  [[ -z $line ]] && continue
  [[ $line == '#'* ]] && continue

  name=${line%%"$tab"*}
  cmd=${line#*"$tab"}
  if [[ $line != *"$tab"* || -z $name || -z $cmd || $name =~ [^A-Za-z0-9_-] ]]; then
    printf 'pool.sh: line %d: malformed\n' "$lineno" >&2
    bad=1
    continue
  fi
  if [[ -v "seen[$name]" ]]; then
    printf 'pool.sh: line %d: duplicate job name: %s\n' "$lineno" "$name" >&2
    bad=1
    continue
  fi
  seen[$name]=1
  names+=("$name")
  cmds+=("$cmd")
done < "$manifest"

(( bad == 0 )) || exit 65
```

# ---- run -------------------------------------------------------------------

```bash
total=0
failed=0
running=0
declare -A job_of_pid=()

# bash >= 5.1 can report which child wait -n reaped; older bash needs a scan.
have_wait_p=0
if (( BASH_VERSINFO[0] > 5 || (BASH_VERSINFO[0] == 5 && BASH_VERSINFO[1] >= 1) )); then
  have_wait_p=1
fi

reap_one() {
  # Block in wait -n until one running job finishes; record its result.
  local pid status name p
  if (( have_wait_p )); then
    wait -n -p pid
    status=$?
  else
    wait -n
    status=$?
    # The reaped child is gone; exited-but-unreaped children are still
    # zombies, so the one pid that fails kill -0 is the one wait took.
    pid=''
    for p in "${!job_of_pid[@]}"; do
      kill -0 "$p" 2>/dev/null || { pid=$p; break; }
    done
  fi
  name=${job_of_pid[$pid]}
  unset 'job_of_pid[$pid]'
  running=$((running - 1))
  printf '%s\t%s\n' "$name" "$status" >> results.txt
  (( status == 0 )) || failed=$((failed + 1))
}

i=0
count=${#names[@]}
while (( i < count )); do
  (( running < limit )) || reap_one
  bash -c "${cmds[$i]}" > "logs/${names[$i]}.log" 2>&1 &
  job_of_pid[$!]=${names[$i]}
  running=$((running + 1))
  total=$((total + 1))
  i=$((i + 1))
done

while (( running > 0 )); do
  reap_one
done

printf 'pool: %d jobs, %d failed\n' "$total" "$failed"
(( failed == 0 ))
```

messages.content:
Done. `bash test_jobpool.sh` passes unmodified — **all 54 checks** — verified across 5 repeat runs (the gated concurrency test is timing-sensitive, so I re-ran to rule out flakiness).

## What I built

`pool.sh` (repo root, `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, bash builtins + coreutils only, no GNU-isms) in three phases:

1. **Arguments** — `$# == 2` and workers matching `^[0-9]+$` with value ≥ 1 (via `10#$2`, so `007` works, `0`/`two`/`+3` don't) → else `usage: ...` on stderr, exit 64. Unreadable manifest → `pool.sh: cannot read: <arg>`, exit 66.
2. **Validate-first** — the whole manifest is read (`IFS= read -r`, with `|| [[ -n $line ]]` so a missing final newline still counts) before anything runs. Split at the *first* tab only (`%%"$tab"*` / `#*"$tab"`), so commands keep embedded tabs/quotes. Missing tab, empty name, empty command, or name chars outside `[A-Za-z0-9_-]` → `pool.sh: line <N>: malformed`; reuse of a valid name → `... duplicate job name: <name>`; physical line numbers, messages in line order, all on stderr. Any bad line → exit 65 with zero side effects (no `logs/`, no `results.txt`, nothing executed).
3. **Run** — `mkdir -p logs`, truncate `results.txt`, then launch jobs in manifest order as `bash -c "$cmd" > logs/<name>.log 2>&1 &` (stdout+stderr share one descriptor, preserving write order). Before each launch, if `running == limit`, **block in `wait -n -p pid`** to free a slot — no polling loop. The reaped PID maps to its name via an assoc array; its true exit status (`$?` from `wait -n`, e.g. `3`, or `143` for signals) is appended as `<name>\t<status>` to `results.txt`. Ends with `pool: <total> jobs, <failed> failed`, exit 0 iff none failed. Empty manifests still create `logs/` + empty `results.txt` and print `pool: 0 jobs, 0 failed`.

## Verification beyond the harness

- 5× clean harness runs; final run after cleanup: `all checks passed (54 checks)`.
- Edge probes: manifest without trailing newline, serial (`limit 1`) ordering, duplicate-on-malformed-line precedence, `+3`/` 3` worker rejection, chmod-000 manifest → exit 66, signaled job recorded as `143`.
- The script targets bash ≥ 5.1's `wait -n -p` but carries a bash 5.0 fallback (post-`wait -n` zombie scan — reaped children fail `kill -0`, unreaped zombies pass). I forced the fallback in a scratch copy and the **full harness passed with it too**, so it's correct on any bash 5.x.

The test file was not touched; the workspace contains only `pool.sh` and the original `test_jobpool.sh`.

messages.content:
=== MOONSHINER TASK BOUNDARY ===

Finance pulls numbers out of our orders export with a pile of copy-pasted jq snippets and no two people get the same answer. Make it a checked-in tool: `report.sh` in the repo root, a thin bash wrapper where jq does all the real work (jq is on every box here), emitting TSV that downstream cut/awk can chew on — build the rows with @tsv so tabs stay tabs.

The export is one JSON document: `{"orders": [...]}`. Each order has `id` (string), optionally `region` (string), optionally `items` (array). Each item has `sku`, `qty`, `unit_price` (integer cents), and optionally `discount` (integer cents off that line, NOT per unit). A line's revenue is `qty * unit_price - discount`, discount defaulting to 0. Fields can be absent at any level — real exports are ragged — and that must never crash the report or leave the word null in the output: a missing `region` is the empty string, a missing `items` is no line items, a missing `orders` key entirely means no orders.

Usage: `report.sh <skus|regions> <file.json>`.

`skus` — one row per distinct sku across every order's items, sorted by sku ascending. Header then rows:

sku<TAB>units<TAB>revenue<TAB>orders

units = summed qty, revenue = summed line revenue, orders = number of DISTINCT order ids that contain the sku (an order listing the same sku on two lines counts once there, but both lines count toward units and revenue).

`regions` — one row per region, orders grouped by `region // ""`, sorted ascending with the empty region first (its row simply starts with the tab). Header then rows:

region<TAB>orders<TAB>units<TAB>revenue

orders = order count in that region (item-less orders still count), units/revenue = sums over those orders' items — a region whose orders have no line items reports 0 and 0, not blanks.

Every count and cent amount prints as a plain integer. Empty report = header line only. Byte-identical output on every run.

Errors: bad invocation (wrong arg count, mode not skus/regions) → `usage: report.sh <skus|regions> <file.json>` on stderr, exit 64. Missing/unreadable file → `report.sh: cannot read: <arg>`, exit 66. File that isn't valid JSON → `report.sh: invalid JSON: <arg>`, exit 65 — and keep jq's own parse spew off the terminal, our one line is the whole story. Success: exit 0, stderr empty.

`bash test_jqreport.sh` from the workspace root is the acceptance gate; it must pass unmodified — don't touch the test file.
